chunk_summary: This section focuses on managing discord and resistance in nutrition counselling using the elicit–provide–elicit method to deliver education while preserving autonomy and reducing defensiveness.
learning_objectives: Understand the elicit–provide–elicit method for delivering education in motivational interviewing.
Learn strategies to manage resistance and discord during nutrition counselling.
Apply reflective techniques to address family disagreements and promote shared outcomes.
Incorporate child-specific motivators into counselling for school-age children and adolescents.
key_facts: The elicit–provide–elicit method involves asking beliefs, sharing targeted information with permission, and exploring its fit in the family context.
Redirecting to shared outcomes and proposing time-limited experiments (e.g., two weeks) can reduce resistance.
Complex reflections, such as acknowledging risks and values, are more effective than direct arguments in managing discord.
Documenting one sentence of change talk (e.g., 'I want fewer fights at meals') helps clarify ownership and guide follow-up.
Direct motivational interviewing for school-age children and adolescents should address motivators like sports stamina, mood, skin health, and menstruation regularity.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: The operational script is elicit–provide–elicit: first ask what the family believes, then ask permission to share targeted information, then ask how it fits their context and what feels doable. (Training New Trainers manual)
document_key_topics: Managing resistance and discord in counselling
Child and adolescent-specific motivators in behavior change
